fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out the automation driver wrapper we've been talking about for the mobile QA platform. Basically right now every time someone wants to run a test session they have to hand-roll a bunch of device setup stuff — figuring out if the session config even makes sense, doing the touch math, flipping network radios, picking the right device, all that. It's a mess and people keep getting it wrong in slightly different ways each time.

The core thing we need is a clean interface that handles all of this internally so test engineers just describe what they want and the driver figures out the rest. Think of it like that capability-checking pattern we used in the login compatibility module — same idea but for device sessions.

Specifically we're getting bug reports about: sessions starting with broken configs and only failing halfway through, gesture coordinates being off especially on drags, network state getting out of sync, locale changes not sticking after device restart, and people not knowing which automation context they're in when switching between native and web views.

Also the ops team is complaining that teardown leaves devices in weird states — keyboards not restored, apps still running, that kind of thing. We need deterministic cleanup.

Output from this thing needs to be totally plain and parseable — no exception class names or runtime garbage in the output, just clean key=value lines. Can someone own this and make sure it handles all the edge cases properly?

trigger_keywords: valid session config
what makes a capability set acceptable
when is a session configuration considered valid
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A capability set is valid when it names at least one launch target: an application file path (app), an installed package identifier (appPackage), or a supported browser (browserName). The platform name is matched case-insensitively. It is rejected with error=missing_required_capability and required=app_or_package_or_browser when no launch target is present; with error=unsupported_browser and the offending browser value when an unsupported browser is named; and with error=conflicting_capabilities and conflict=app_and_browser when both an app file and a browser are specified simultaneously. A valid configuration outputs valid=true.

trigger_keywords: proxy avoid list
which routes should never be forwarded
what endpoints are always handled locally
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The proxy avoid list contains exactly 8 entries in this order: POST ^/session/[^/]+/context, GET ^/session/[^/]+/context, POST ^/session/[^/]+/appium, GET ^/session/[^/]+/appium, POST ^/session/[^/]+/touch/perform, POST ^/session/[^/]+/touch/multi/perform, POST ^/session/[^/]+/orientation, GET ^/session/[^/]+/orientation. Each is output as: avoid_route method=<METHOD> pattern=<REGEX>. Until an external web engine is attached, proxy_active=false.

trigger_keywords: moveTo relative coordinates
how does touch gesture position accumulate
gesture coordinate resolution for moveTo
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A moveTo action accumulates onto the running position from the previous state — it is not absolute. So if the current position is (100,101) and a moveTo specifies x=50, y=51, the resolved position becomes (150,152). A subsequent moveTo with x=-40, y=-41 resolves to (110,111). A wait action inherits the current running position and emits x and y. A release action emits no coordinates at all.

trigger_keywords: drag duration default
how long does a long press drag last
minimum drag duration by api level
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The drag duration defaults to a platform-dependent minimum: 2 seconds on platforms at API level 5 and above, and 1 second on platforms below API level 5. If the long-press specifies an explicit hold duration in milliseconds, it is converted to seconds and used only when it exceeds the platform minimum; otherwise the minimum wins. Output fields are start_x, start_y, end_x, end_y, and duration (in whole seconds).

trigger_keywords: network bitmask encoding
how is airplane mode represented in the connection value
what does the connection integer mean
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The connection bitmask uses bit value 1 for airplane mode, 2 for WiFi, and 4 for mobile data. When airplane mode is on, the result is exactly 1 and WiFi/data bits are not added. Otherwise the result is the sum of active WiFi and data bits. When applying a bitmask, airplane mode is always set and broadcast first. When airplane mode is requested on (bit 1 set), processing stops and WiFi/data are not touched. When airplane mode is off, set_wifi_and_data is called with the decoded wifi and data flags.

trigger_keywords: toggle location services key sequence
what keys are sent for location toggle
api level branching for location settings
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: API level 15 and below have no global location toggle and return error=not_implemented. API level 16 opens LOCATION_SOURCE_SETTINGS and sends key sequence 19,19,20. API level 19 and above sends a preparatory pre_keyevent=19 before opening LOCATION_SOURCE_SETTINGS, then sends key sequence 22,22,19. Output format: optional pre_keyevent line, then setting=LOCATION_SOURCE_SETTINGS, then key_sequence=<comma-separated codes>.

trigger_keywords: locale change reboot
when does device reboot after locale update
api level 23 locale versus separate language country
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Below API level 23, language and country are independent settings. Only differing values are written using device_op=set_language and device_op=set_country, and a reboot follows any change. At API level 23 and above, a single combined locale is used formatted as lowercase-language + dash + uppercase-country (e.g. en-US), written with device_op=set_locale. A reboot follows if the value changed. When neither language nor country is requested, or when the current values already match, output is no_change=true.

trigger_keywords: which device gets selected
how is the target device resolved from capabilities
device not found error condition
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a specific udid is requested it must appear in the connected device list; if not found, output error=device_not_found with the requested udid value. When no udid is requested, the first connected device is chosen. When an AVD virtual device name is requested (avd capability), the running emulator's identifier and port are used. Output fields are udid and emulator_port.

trigger_keywords: remote apk staging path
where is the app cached on device
content hash path for apk
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The canonical on-device staging path is constructed as /data/local/tmp/<md5hash>.apk — the device temporary directory followed by the content hash and an .apk suffix. Output is remote_path=<path>.

trigger_keywords: get strings caching
when are localized strings re-extracted
language fallback for string lookup
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the requested language is already in the cache, the cached map is returned without re-extracting. When no language is requested, the device's current language is used to select from the cache. When the language is not cached, strings are freshly extracted and returned. Each string is output as key=value on its own line.

trigger_keywords: remove null properties behavior
which property values are kept when filtering
false and zero kept after null removal
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Only properties whose value is null or undefined are removed. Falsy-but-defined values such as false and 0 are kept. Output lists remaining_keys as a comma-separated list in original order, and key_count as the integer count of remaining properties.

trigger_keywords: automation contexts available
native versus web context enumeration
chromium context name for browser sessions
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The native context (NATIVE_APP) is always listed first. When the session targets an in-app web engine browser such as Chrome, the web engine context CHROMIUM is also listed. Otherwise available web views discovered from the device are appended. Output is contexts=<comma-separated list>.

trigger_keywords: session teardown order
what happens during cleanup when keyboard was changed
full reset uninstall during teardown
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If alreadyShutDown is true, output already_shut_down=true and stop. Otherwise: if unicodeKeyboard and resetKeyboard are both true and a defaultIME was recorded, restore it first with device_op=set_ime value=<ime>. Then always device_op=force_stop, then device_op=go_to_home. If fullReset is true, additionally device_op=uninstall_apk at the end. Operations are always emitted in this fixed order.

trigger_keywords: java version parsing
how is the jdk version detected from output
unrecognized java banner error
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The parser scans raw banner text for a line containing a Java or OpenJDK version banner and extracts the version token from within quotes. When recognized, output is java_version=<version>. When the banner cannot be recognized, output is error=java_version_unavailable. No host-language exception details appear in output.

trigger_keywords: gesture routing element scoped
difference between element swipe and bare swipe command
how is command name determined for flick drag swipe
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a gesture (swipe, flick, or drag) is scoped to a specific element, the resolved command name is element:<gesture> (e.g. element:swipe). When not scoped to an element, the bare command name is used (e.g. swipe). Output is command=<resolved_name>.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what values can op take
list all supported op values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'validate_capabilities', 'proxy_config', 'parse_touch', 'gesture_route', 'get_network', 'set_network', 'toggle_location', 'ensure_locale', 'device_info', 'java_version', 'remote_apk_path', 'get_strings', 'remove_null_properties', 'get_contexts', 'session_teardown'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: that capability-checking pattern we used in the login compatibility module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 1 capability validation logic in feature1_capability_validation.json — the three rejection conditions (missing launch target, unsupported browser, conflicting app+browser) and the valid=true success path

context_pointers.vague_reference: gesture coordinates being off especially on drags
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 3.1 moveTo relative coordinate accumulation (feature3_1_parse_touch_absolute.json) and Feature 3.2 drag duration computation with API-level-dependent minimums (feature3_2_touch_drag_duration.json)

context_pointers.vague_reference: locale changes not sticking after device restart
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 5.1 ensure_locale logic in feature5_1_ensure_device_locale.json — the API level 23 branch uses a single combined locale string; below 23 uses separate set_language/set_country ops; reboot is only emitted when a value actually changed

context_pointers.vague_reference: keyboards not restored, apps still running
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 9 session teardown in feature9_session_teardown.json — the fixed teardown order: optional IME restore (only when unicodeKeyboard+resetKeyboard+defaultIME all present), force_stop, go_to_home, optional uninstall_apk for fullReset

context_pointers.vague_reference: not knowing which automation context they're in when switching between native and web views
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 8.1 get_contexts (feature8_1_get_contexts.json) and Feature 2 proxy avoid list (feature2_3_proxy_avoid_list.json) — context switching endpoints must never be proxied, and NATIVE_APP is always the first listed context

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I can confirm from the current ticket description. You'll need to check the test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ or the start.md PRD for the precise behavior — specifically look at the relevant feature section for the operation you're asking about.

feature1_capability_validation.json.description: Validate a requested capability set before a session starts. The validator accepts a configuration that names at least one launch target (an application file path, an installed package identifier, or a supported browser) and is case-insensitive about the platform name. It rejects a configuration that names no launch target, names an unsupported browser, or names both an application file and a browser at the same time. A valid configuration outputs valid=true; a rejected one outputs a neutral error category plus any offending value.

feature2_1_proxy_active.json.description: Report whether request proxying to an external in-app web engine is currently active. Until such an engine is attached, proxying is inactive, so this query returns proxy_active=false.

feature2_3_proxy_avoid_list.json.description: Expose the list of HTTP method + URL-pattern pairs that must never be proxied to an external web engine and are always handled locally. Each pair names a method and a route regular expression. The list covers context switching, vendor-specific extension endpoints, touch performance endpoints, and orientation endpoints.

cases.expected_output:
avoid_route method=POST pattern=^\/session\/[^/]+\/context
avoid_route method=GET pattern=^\/session\/[^/]+\/context
avoid_route method=POST pattern=^\/session\/[^/]+\/appium
avoid_route method=GET pattern=^\/session\/[^/]+\/appium
avoid_route method=POST pattern=^\/session\/[^/]+\/touch\/perform
avoid_route method=POST pattern=^\/session\/[^/]+\/touch\/multi\/perform
avoid_route method=POST pattern=^\/session\/[^/]+\/orientation
avoid_route method=GET pattern=^\/session\/[^/]+\/orientation

feature3_1_parse_touch_absolute.json.description: Normalize a touch gesture sequence given in absolute screen coordinates into a list of resolved touch states. Each press/moveTo/tap/longPress state carries an absolute x/y; a moveTo expressed relative to the previous position is accumulated onto the running position; a wait state inherits the current position; and a release state carries no coordinates. The output lists each state in order with its resolved coordinates.

cases.expected_output:
action=press x=100 y=101
action=moveTo x=150 y=152
action=wait x=150 y=152
action=moveTo x=110 y=111
action=release

feature3_3_gesture_routing.json.description: Route a gesture to the correct underlying device command name depending on whether it is scoped to a specific element or applied at screen level. A swipe, flick, or drag scoped to an element emits an element-prefixed command; the same gesture without an element emits the bare command. The output is the resolved command name.

feature4_1_get_network_connection.json.description: Compute the current network connection state as a bitmask from three independent device signals: airplane mode (bit value 1), WiFi (bit value 2), and mobile data (bit value 4). When airplane mode is on the result is 1 and the WiFi and data bits are not added. Otherwise the result is the sum of the WiFi and data bits that are on. The output is the integer connection value.

feature4_2_set_network_connection.json.description: Apply a requested network connection state given as a bitmask, decoding it into ordered device operations. The lowest bit selects airplane mode, the next bit WiFi, the next bit data. The system always sets and broadcasts airplane mode first. When airplane mode is requested on, it stops there and does not touch WiFi/data. When airplane mode is off, it then applies the WiFi and data flags together. The output lists each device operation in order with its arguments.

cases.expected_output:
device_op=set_airplane_mode value=0
device_op=broadcast_airplane_mode value=0
device_op=set_wifi_and_data wifi=0 data=0

device_op=set_airplane_mode value=0
device_op=broadcast_airplane_mode value=0
device_op=set_wifi_and_data wifi=1 data=0

device_op=set_airplane_mode value=0
device_op=broadcast_airplane_mode value=0
device_op=set_wifi_and_data wifi=0 data=1

device_op=set_airplane_mode value=0
device_op=broadcast_airplane_mode value=0
device_op=set_wifi_and_data wifi=1 data=1

feature4_3_toggle_location_services.json.description: Toggle the device location-services setting by opening the relevant settings screen and emitting a platform-dependent key navigation sequence to move focus onto the toggle. Platforms at API level 16 use the sequence 19,19,20 (up, up, down). Platforms at API level 19 and above use 22,22,19 (right, right, up) and additionally send one preparatory up keyevent (19) before opening the screen. Platforms at API level 15 and below have no global toggle and report a not_implemented error.

feature5_1_ensure_device_locale.json.description: Ensure the device language and country match a requested target, performing the minimum device operations needed and rebooting only when something changed. When neither language nor country is requested, nothing happens. On platforms below API level 23, language and country are independent settings: only the ones that differ from the current value are written, and a reboot follows any change. On platforms at API level 23 and above the value is a single combined locale (lowercase-language + dash + uppercase-country): it is rewritten only if it differs, followed by a reboot. The output lists each device operation in order, or no_change when nothing was needed.

feature5_3_device_info_from_caps.json.description: Resolve which connected device to use and report its identifier and emulator port. When a specific device identifier is requested it must appear in the connected device list, otherwise a device_not_found error is reported along with the requested identifier. When no identifier is requested, the first connected device is chosen. When a virtual device name is requested, the running emulator's identifier and port are used. The output gives the resolved udid and emulator_port.

feature5_5_java_version.json.description: Detect the installed Java runtime version from the raw banner text a Java toolchain prints. The parser scans for a line containing a Java or OpenJDK version banner and extracts the version token. When the banner is recognized, the version is reported; when it cannot be recognized, the operation reports a neutral java_version_unavailable error.

feature6_1_remote_apk_path.json.description: Compute the canonical on-device staging path for a cached application archive from its content hash. The path is the device temporary directory followed by the hash and an .apk suffix.

feature7_1_get_strings.json.description: Return the localized application strings for a requested language. When the requested language is already cached, the cached map is returned without extracting again. When no language is requested, the device's current language is used to select the cached map. When the language is not cached, the strings are freshly extracted and returned. The output lists each string key=value pair.

feature7_3_remove_null_properties.json.description: Remove properties whose value is null or undefined from an object, while keeping properties whose value is falsy-but-defined such as false or 0. The output lists the remaining keys in order and their count.

feature8_1_get_contexts.json.description: Enumerate the automation contexts currently available. The native context is always present and listed first. When the session targets an in-app web engine browser, the web engine context is also listed. Otherwise the available web views are discovered from the device and appended. The output lists the contexts as a comma-separated set.

feature9_session_teardown.json.description: Tear down a session, performing only the device operations appropriate to its options and in a fixed order. If the session was never started (already shut down), nothing is done beyond noting that. Otherwise, when the unicode keyboard was enabled with keyboard reset and a default input method was recorded, the input method is restored first. A non-web session is force-stopped, then the device is sent to its home screen. When full reset is requested, the application is additionally uninstalled. The output lists each device operation in order.
